## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in FMS

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N_{p}$ | Number of part types in FMS | unitless | **20–200** |
| $N_{m}$ | Number of machines | unitless | **4–20** |
| $B$ | Buffer capacity | pallets | **10–100** |
| $T_{c}$ | Average changeover time | minutes | **<15 min** |
| $\eta_{sys}$ | System utilization | percentage | **70%–85%** |
| $TH$ | Throughput | parts/hour | **5–30 parts/h** |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours | **0.5–4 h** |
| $MTBF$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours | **100–500 h** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *Machine Flexibility* with *Routing Flexibility*. Machine flexibility is about *what a single machine can do*; Routing flexibility is about *alternate paths through the system*. Examiners test this distinction frequently.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Workstations — The Manufacturing Core

A **workstation** in an FMS is a CNC machine tool, inspection station, or assembly cell equipped with automatic tool changers, pallet changers, and DNC interfacing, capable of unattended operation for extended periods.

### 2.1.1 Workstation Functional Architecture

Every workstation contains the following sub-elements:

- **CNC Controller** (Fanuc, Siemens, Mitsubishi) — receives NC code from cell controller via DNC link.
- **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)** — magazine of **20–120 tools** with random-selection capability.
- **Pallet Changer (APC)** — permits workpiece swap without operator intervention.
- **Workpiece Identification Sensor** — barcode/RFID reader for closed-loop verification.
- **In-Process Gauging Probe** (Renishaw OMP60) — measures parts between operations.
- **Coolant/Chip Management System** — supports lights-out machining.

### 2.1.2 Workstation Classification (KTU Mandate)

| Type | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Machining Workstation** | CNC lathe, machining center, EDM | Bulk material removal |
| **Assembly Workstation** | SCARA/6-axis robot with parts feeders | Sub-assemblies |
| **Inspection Workstation** | CMM, vision system, hardness tester | Quality assurance |
| **Washing/De-burring Station** | Ultrasonic cleaner, robotic deburr | Surface finishing |
| **Sheet Metal Workstation** | CNC press brake, laser cutter | Sheet components |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 80/20 Rule for Workstation Selection:** Roughly **80% of FMS part variety** is handled by **20% of workstation types** (typically machining centers + lathes). This Pareto insight is often asked in KTU descriptive answers.

### 2.1.3 KTU Workstation Performance Equations

**Throughput Time at Workstation (Little's Law application):**

$$T_{ws} = W_{q} + T_{s}$$

Where:
- $T_{ws}$ = total time workpiece spends at workstation
- $W_{q}$ = waiting time in queue (before processing)
- $T_{s}$ = actual processing/setup time

**Workstation Utilization:**

$$\eta_{ws} = \frac{T_{s}}{T_{ws}} = \frac{T_{s}}{W_{q} + T_{s}}$$

**MTBF-driven Availability:**

$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

---

## 2.2 Material Handling and Storage Systems (MH&SS)

### 2.2.1 Material Handling System (MHS) — The Circulatory System

The MHS is the **physical transport network** that moves workpieces, tooling, and pallets between workstations, storage, and assembly cells. Selection of MHS dictates FMS flexibility, cost, and scalability.

**KTU Classification of MHS:**

1. **Conveyor-based** (belt, roller, chain) — high throughput, low flexibility
2. **Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV)** — wire-guided or laser-guided, moderate flexibility
3. **Robotic/Articulated Arm** — used in cells, very high flexibility, short range
4. **Tow-line / In-floor Tractor** — continuous loop, heavy payloads
5. **Overhead Monorail / Power-and-Free** — unidirectional loop, gravity return
6. **Cartesian Gantry Robot** — bridge-type, large work envelopes

### 2.2.2 MHS Selection Criteria (Engineering Decision Matrix)

| Criterion | Weight | Conveyor | AGV | Robot | Tow-line |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Throughput (parts/h) | 0.20 | **High** | Medium | Medium | **High** |
| Flexibility (routes) | 0.20 | Low | **High** | Medium | Low |
| Initial Cost (₹) | 0.15 | Medium | **High** | Medium | High |
| Floor Space Req. | 0.10 | Low | Low | **Low** | High |
| Maintenance | 0.15 | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| Scalability | 0.10 | Low | **High** | Medium | Low |
| Reliability (MTBF) | 0.10 | **High** | Medium | Medium | High |

### 2.2.3 Storage Systems — The Decoupling Buffer

The storage system is the **decoupling element** that absorbs stochastic variations in machine cycle times and part demand, preventing cascading stoppages across the FMS.

**KTU Storage System Hierarchy:**

- **Centralized AS/RS** — single high-rise crane-served rack (50,000+ pallets)
- **Distributed Buffer** — small racks at each machine (5–10 pallets each)
- **Tool Magazine / Tool Pool** — holds cutting tools centrally (300–2000 tools)
- **Fixtures/Pallet Pool** — modular fixturing + standardized pallets

**Pallet Fixturing Standard:**
The key enabler of FMS is the **standardized pallet**. Almost all KTU 2024 questions require understanding that a single pallet (typically **400 mm × 400 mm** or **500 mm × 500 mm**) can mount multiple part types via modular fixture plates, enabling *random arrival of parts to a machine*.

### 2.2.4 KTU MHS and Storage Formula Sheet

| Formula | Symbolic Form | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AGV Fleet Size** | $N_{AGV} = \lceil T_{cycle} \times \lambda / 3600 \rceil$ | $T_{cycle}$ = avg cycle time (s); $\lambda$ = demand rate (parts/h) | Fleet sizing |
| **Conveyor Capacity** | $C_{conv} = v_{belt} \times \rho_{pallet}$ | $v$ = belt speed (m/min); $\rho$ = pallet density (pallets/m) | Throughput check |
| **AS/RS Throughput** | $TH_{AS/RS} = 3600 \times 2 \times N_{crane} / (2 T_{s} + 2 T_{h})$ | $T_{s}$ = horizontal travel (s); $T_{h}$ = vertical travel (s) | Crane Sizing |
| **Buffer Level (Kanban)** | $B = D \times L \times (1 + S)$ | $D$ = demand rate; $L$ = lead time; $S$ = safety factor | Buffer design |
| **Storage Density** | $\eta_{store} = V_{stored} / V_{footprint}$ | Unitless ratio | Layout efficiency |
| **Pallet Pool Size** | $N_{pallet} = N_{machines} \times 1.5$ | Empirical | Practical sizing |
| **Hoist Travel Time (AS/RS)** | $T_{h} = 2 H / v_{hoist}$ | $H$ = rack height (m); $v_{hoist}$ = vertical speed (m/s) | Crane cycle |
| **Order Pick Time** | $T_{p} = T_{f} + T_{t} + T_{r}$ | $T_{f}$ = fetch; $T_{t}$ = travel; $T_{r}$ = return | Operator/MHS interaction |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Heuristic:** The AS/RS throughput formula is *always* twice divided by a round-trip time, because one crane cycle performs *one* retrieval *and* *one* storage (dual command cycle).

---

## 2.3 Computer Control Systems — The Central Nervous System

### 2.3.1 Hierarchical Control Architecture (KTU Mandate)

The FMS computer control system is a **multi-level pyramid**, with each level handling a specific time-scale and decision-type:

| Level | Function | Typical Equipment | Time Scale |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L4 — Enterprise** | ERP, order management, finance | SAP/Oracle servers | Days–Months |
| **L3 — Shop Floor / MES** | Production scheduling, dispatch | Manufacturing Execution System | Hours–Days |
| **L2 — Cell / FMS Host** | Real-time routing, sequencing | Industrial PC + SCADA | Minutes–Hours |
| **L1 — Workstation / DNC** | NC code download, tool mgmt | CNC controller | Seconds–Minutes |
| **L0 — Sensors/Actuators** | Field I/O, position feedback | PLCs, transducers | Milliseconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISA-95 Reference Model:** KTU 2024 expects students to mention the **ISA-95 framework** when describing the FMS control hierarchy. It is the international standard for enterprise-control system integration.

### 2.3.2 Functions of the FMS Host Computer

- **Order Release & Dispatch** — decides which part enters the system and when.
- **Routing & Scheduling** — assigns each operation to a specific machine; solves job-shop scheduling problem in real-time.
- **Tool Management** — tracks tool wear, schedules regrinds, manages tool pool.
- **Pallet/Fixture Management** — tracks part location in queue.
- **DNC (Direct Numerical Control)** — distributed NC code distribution to machines.
- **Shop-Floor Data Collection (SFDC)** — captures cycle time, alarms, throughput.
- **Quality Data Integration** — links CMM data with process control.
- **Maintenance Management** — schedules preventive maintenance based on MTBF.

### 2.3.3 Networking Protocols in FMS

- **Fieldbus**: Profibus, DeviceNet, Modbus, EtherCAT
- **Ethernet/IP**: Standard plant-floor LAN (100 Mbps–1 Gbps)
- **OPC UA**: Modern data-exchange standard (cross-platform)
- **MTConnect**: Specifically for machine tool telemetry

### 2.3.4 KTU Control System Performance Formulas

**Dispatching Priority Index (Shortest Processing Time rule):**

$$P_{SPT}(i) = \min_{j \in Q_i} \{ p_{ij} \}$$

Where $p_{ij}$ is the processing time of part $i$ on machine $j$.

**WIP via Little's Law:**

$$WIP = TH \times T_{lead}$$

**Scheduling Slack (Critical Ratio):**

$$CR = \frac{T_{due} - T_{now}}{T_{remaining}}$$

If $CR \leq 1.0$, job is *behind schedule*.

---

## 2.4 Human Resources — The Cognitive Layer

### 2.4.1 Human Roles in an FMS

The "unmanned factory" myth is false. Humans are essential for:

- **System Management** — Production engineers set schedules, monitor KPIs.
- **CNC Programming & Process Engineering** — NC code generation, tool selection.
- **Operations** — Pallet loading, first-piece inspection, exception handling.
- **Maintenance** — Predictive, preventive, and corrective actions.
- **Quality Assurance** — SPC charting, CMM programming, root-cause analysis.
- **Tool Crib Management** — Tool kitting, regrind scheduling.

### 2.4.2 Required Skill Matrix (KTU-Approved)

| Role | Key Skills | Training (hrs/year) |
|---|---|---|
| CNC Operator | G-code, pallet handling, basic CMM | **40–80** |
| FMS Supervisor | Scheduling software, SCADA, Lean | **80–120** |
| Maintenance Tech | Hydraulics, PLC, mechatronics | **120–200** |
| Process Engineer | CAM, tool design, GD&T | **200+** |
| Quality Engineer | SPC, Minitab, Six-Sigma Black Belt | **200+** |

### 2.4.3 Operator-to-Machine Ratio (OMR) — KTU Metric

$$OMR = \frac{N_{operators}}{N_{machines}}$$

For an FMS, the **typical OMR is 0.2 to 0.5**, meaning each operator oversees 2–5 machines. This is a **5–10× improvement** over conventional CNC cells (OMR ≈ 1.0–2.0).

> [!NOTE]
> **Human Factors Engineering Principle:** The "5:1:1" rule of FMS staffing — for every **5 machines**, there is **1 operator** and **1 maintenance technician**. This is a frequently tested ratio in KTU 2024 numerical problems.

---

## 2.5 Why These Components Matter — Real-World Engineering Utility

| Component | Industrial Application | Iconic Example |
|---|---|---|
| Workstations | Aerospace component machining | Boeing 737 engine mounts |
| MHS | Auto parts transfer | Tesla Gigafactory AGVs |
| AS/RS | E-commerce fulfillment | Amazon Kiva robots |
| Control | Real-time shop floor decisions | Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl |
| Human | Exception handling, creative work | Mazak's iSMART Factory |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Buffer Capacity in an FMS Queue

We want to derive the **minimum buffer size $B_{min}$** between two tandem machines such that blocking is avoided with probability $\geq 0.95$.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Two machines M1 and M2 are connected by a buffer of capacity $B$. M1 cycle time $t_1 \sim N(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)$ and M2 cycle time $t_2 \sim N(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)$, with both normally distributed and statistically independent.

**Step 1:** Define the *differential cycle time* as the random variable:

$$\Delta t = t_1 - t_2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the linearity of the Normal distribution. The difference of two independent normal random variables is also normal, with mean and variance:

$$\mu_{\Delta} = \mu_1 - \mu_2$$

$$\sigma_{\Delta}^2 = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2$$

This is because variances always add, regardless of sign.

**Step 3:** Standardize by converting to a standard normal variable $Z$:

$$Z = \frac{\Delta t - \mu_{\Delta}}{\sigma_{\Delta}} = \frac{(t_1 - t_2) - (\mu_1 - \mu_2)}{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}}$$

**Step 4:** We want the probability that M1 produces *at most* $B$ parts before M2 consumes them (no blocking):

$$P(\Delta t \leq T_{buffer}) \geq 0.95$$

where $T_{buffer} = B \times \mu_2$ (the time M2 takes to clear $B$ parts).

**Step 5:** From the standard normal table, $P(Z \leq 1.645) = 0.95$. Therefore:

$$\frac{B \cdot \mu_2 - (\mu_1 - \mu_2)}{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}} = 1.645$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $B$ algebraically:

$$B \cdot \mu_2 = 1.645 \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2} + \mu_1 - \mu_2$$

**Step 7:** Final result — minimum buffer capacity:

$$B_{min} = \frac{1.645 \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2} + \mu_1 - \mu_2}{\mu_2}$$

### 3.1.2 Numerical Example

Given: $\mu_1 = 12$ min, $\sigma_1 = 1.5$ min, $\mu_2 = 11$ min, $\sigma_2 = 1.0$ min.

Compute $\sigma_{\Delta} = \sqrt{1.5^2 + 1.0^2} = \sqrt{2.25 + 1.00} = \sqrt{3.25} = 1.803$ min.

Compute numerator: $1.645 \times 1.803 + (12 - 11) = 2.966 + 1 = 3.966$ min.

Compute $B_{min} = 3.966 / 11 = 0.361$.

Since $B$ must be an integer, **round up** to $B = 1$ pallet. In practice, a safety factor of 1.5–2× is applied, so $B_{design} = 2$ pallets.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: AGV Fleet Size Optimization

### 3.2.1 Objective Function

Minimize the number of AGVs $N_{AGV}$ subject to a service level constraint (no part waits longer than $T_{max}$).

**Step 1:** Define AGV service rate per vehicle (parts per hour):

$$\mu_{AGV} = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}}$$

where $T_{cycle}$ = average round-trip time of one AGV (in seconds).

**Step 2:** Define the part demand rate $\lambda$ (parts/h) imposed by all machines.

**Step 3:** Apply the basic traffic-intensity inequality. The system is stable if total AGV capacity exceeds demand:

$$N_{AGV} \cdot \mu_{AGV} \geq \lambda$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the lower bound:

$$N_{AGV} \geq \frac{\lambda \cdot T_{cycle}}{3600}$$

**Step 5:** Add a safety factor $\alpha$ (typically 0.2–0.3) to account for AGV queuing, charging, and breakdowns:

$$N_{AGV} = \left\lceil \frac{\lambda \cdot T_{cycle}}{3600} \cdot (1 + \alpha) \right\rceil$$

**Step 6:** Numerical example. Suppose $\lambda = 60$ parts/h and $T_{cycle} = 240$ s, $\alpha = 0.25$.

Compute: $N_{AGV} = \lceil (60 \times 240 / 3600) \times 1.25 \rceil = \lceil 4.0 \times 1.25 \rceil = \lceil 5.0 \rceil = 5$ AGVs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: AS/RS Crane Throughput (Dual-Command Cycle)

A single crane serves a rack of height $H$ and width $W$, with horizontal speed $v_h$ and vertical speed $v_v$. A *dual-command* cycle performs one storage *and* one retrieval in one round trip.

**Step 1:** The horizontal travel distance (worst case) is the full rack width:

$$D_h = W$$

The vertical travel (worst case) is the full rack height:

$$D_v = H$$

**Step 2:** The combined travel time, assuming simultaneous horizontal + vertical motion (Chebyshev travel):

$$T_{travel} = \max\left(\frac{W}{v_h}, \frac{H}{v_v}\right) + \frac{\min(W, H)}{\min(v_h, v_v)}$$

**Step 3:** Add pickup/deposit time $T_{p+d}$ (typically 5–10 s per action). For a dual command, two actions are performed:

$$T_{p+d}^{total} = 2 T_{p+d}$$

**Step 4:** Total dual-command cycle time:

$$T_{cycle} = 2 T_{travel} + 2 T_{p+d}$$

**Step 5:** Throughput of one crane (transactions/hour):

$$TH_{crane} = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}/2} \cdot 1 = \frac{7200}{T_{cycle}}$$

Wait — careful! Each dual-command cycle completes 2 transactions. So:

$$TH_{crane} = \frac{3600 \times 2}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{7200}{T_{cycle}}$$

**Step 6:** For $N_c$ identical cranes in parallel:

$$TH_{AS/RS} = N_c \cdot \frac{7200}{T_{cycle}}$$

**Step 7:** Numerical example: $W = 30$ m, $H = 12$ m, $v_h = 2$ m/s, $v_v = 1$ m/s, $T_{p+d} = 8$ s.

Compute: $W/v_h = 15$ s, $H/v_v = 12$ s. Max = 15 s. Min = 12 s; min speed = 1, so add $12/1 = 12$ s. $T_{travel} = 15 + 12 = 27$ s.

$T_{cycle} = 2(27) + 2(8) = 54 + 16 = 70$ s.

$TH_{crane} = 7200/70 = 102.86$ transactions/h.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance Table

| FMS Type | Machines | MHS | Storage | Human OMR | Typical Industry |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I — Dedicated FMS** | 4–6 machining centers | AGV + Conveyor | Central AS/RS | 0.25 | Aerospace |
| **Type II — Random FMS** | 8–20 mixed | AGV + Robot | Distributed | 0.40 | Automotive |
| **Type III — Flexible Cell** | 2–3 machines | Robot arm | Local buffer | 0.50 | Job shop |
| **Type IV — Modular FMS** | Scalable 5–50 | Modular AGV | Modular AS/RS | 0.30 | Contract mfg |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Examples (KTU-Style)

### Example 1: FMS Utilization Calculation
An FMS has 8 machines operating 16 hours/day. Each machine averages 90% uptime and processes 4 parts/h. Calculate:
- (a) System throughput $TH$
- (b) Daily production $N_d$
- (c) Operator requirement if OMR = 0.4

**Solution:**

(a) $TH = 8 \times 4 \times 0.90 = 28.8$ parts/h

(b) $N_d = 28.8 \times 16 = 460.8 \approx 460$ parts/day

(c) $N_{op} = 8 \times 0.4 = 3.2 \Rightarrow 4$ operators (round up)

### Example 2: Buffer Sizing Re-verification
Reusing Section 3.1.2: with $B_{design} = 2$ pallets, what is the *probability of blocking*?

We compute the Z-score when $B = 2$:

$$Z = \frac{2 \times 11 - (12 - 11)}{1.803} = \frac{22 - 1}{1.803} = \frac{21}{1.803} = 11.65$$

A Z-score of 11.65 corresponds to probability $\approx 1 - 10^{-31}$, essentially zero blocking. Hence $B = 2$ is heavily over-designed, confirming the safety-factor heuristic.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FMS Top-Level Architecture — Block Diagram

## 4.2 FMS Information and Physical Flow — ISA-95 Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef lv4 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef lv3 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef lv2 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef lv1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef lv0 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000,color:#000

    L4[Level 4 Enterprise - ERP]:::lv4
    L3[Level 3 Operations - MES]:::lv3
    L2[Level 2 Supervisory - Cell Controller]:::lv2
    L1[Level 1 Control - CNC and PLC]:::lv1
    L0[Level 0 Field - Sensors and Actuators]:::lv0

    L4 -->|Order Release| L3
    L3 -->|Production Schedule| L2
    L2 -->|NC Code and Dispatch| L1
    L1 -->|Pulse Trains and Switches| L0
    L0 -->|Telemetry and Feedback| L1
    L1 -->|Cycle Time and Alarms| L2
    L2 -->|KPI Reports| L3
    L3 -->|OEE Dashboards| L4
```

## 4.3 Workstation Internal Architecture


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS). List its five major components.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) is a computer-controlled, integrated production system that combines the flexibility of a job shop with the throughput efficiency of a flow line, capable of producing a variety of part types in small to medium batch sizes.
>
> **Five major components:**
> 1. **Workstations** — CNC machines, inspection stations, assembly cells.
> 2. **Material Handling System (MHS)** — AGVs, conveyors, robots.
> 3. **Storage Systems** — AS/RS, tool pools, pallet pools.
> 4. **Computer Control System** — Cell controllers, DNC, MES.
> 5. **Human Resources** — Operators, supervisors, maintenance, engineers.
>
> (1 mark for definition + 2 marks for 5 components with 0.4 each ≈ 2 marks for components)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Distinguish between *Machine Flexibility* and *Routing Flexibility* in an FMS context.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Machine Flexibility | Routing Flexibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Range of part types a single machine can produce without re-tooling | Ability of the system to re-route work through alternate machines |
| **Scope** | Single workstation | Entire FMS network |
| **Measured by** | Number of part types per machine | Number of alternate operation paths per part |
| **Affected by** | Tool magazine capacity, NC programming | Material handling, software scheduling, buffer size |
| **Improvement lever** | ATC, multi-axis CNC | AGV flexibility, MES routing algorithms |
| (1½ marks) | (1½ marks) |

(½ mark for each of three correct points per side = 3 marks total)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Describe in detail the **architecture and functions of the computer control system** in an FMS, with a neat block diagram of the ISA-95 hierarchy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An FMS has 6 CNC machines. The average cycle time per part is 12 minutes. Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) is 200 hours and Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) is 4 hours. The system operates for 16 hours/day. Calculate: **(i)** Availability of each machine, **(ii)** System throughput per day, **(iii)** Minimum buffer size to avoid 95% blocking, given $\sigma_1 = 1.5$ min and $\sigma_2 = 1.2$ min. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of FMS Computer Control System (7 Marks):**

The FMS computer control system follows the **ISA-95 hierarchical model** with five levels:

- **Level 4 – Enterprise (ERP):** Handles business functions — order management, finance, supply chain. Examples: SAP, Oracle. Time scale: days to months. *(1 mark)*
- **Level 3 – Operations (MES):** Production scheduling, dispatch, detailed routing, quality tracking. Examples: Siemens Opcenter, Rockwell FactoryTalk. Time scale: hours to days. *(1 mark)*
- **Level 2 – Supervisory (FMS Host / Cell Controller):** Real-time scheduling, DNC code distribution, tool management, alarm handling. Industrial PC with SCADA software. Time scale: minutes to hours. *(1 mark)*
- **Level 1 – Control (CNC / PLC):** Machine-level NC execution, tool changer, pallet changer, spindle control. Examples: Fanuc 30i, Siemens 840D sl. Time scale: seconds. *(1 mark)*
- **Level 0 – Field (Sensors/Actuators):** Proximity switches, encoders, RFID readers, hydraulic valves. Time scale: milliseconds. *(1 mark)*

**Functions of FMS Host (Cell Controller):**
- Order release and part tracking
- Real-time routing and scheduling
- NC code distribution via DNC
- Tool life monitoring and regrind scheduling
- Pallet and fixture tracking
- Shop Floor Data Collection (SFDC)
- Maintenance scheduling

*(2 marks for the neat hierarchical diagram and function list)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Machine Availability (2 marks):**

$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{200}{200 + 4} = \frac{200}{204} = 0.9804 = 98.04\%$$

**(ii) System Throughput (2 marks):**

Effective machine production time = 16 h × 0.9804 = 15.69 h/day

Parts per machine per day = 15.69 × 60 / 12 = 78.43 parts/day

System throughput = 6 × 78.43 = 470.6 ≈ **470 parts/day** *(full 2 marks)*

**(iii) Minimum Buffer Size (3 marks):**

Apply the formula derived in Section 3.1.7:

$$B_{min} = \frac{1.645 \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2} + \mu_1 - \mu_2}{\mu_2}$$

Step-by-step:
- $\sigma_1 = 1.5$ min, $\sigma_2 = 1.2$ min
- $\sigma_\Delta = \sqrt{1.5^2 + 1.2^2} = \sqrt{2.25 + 1.44} = \sqrt{3.69} = 1.921$ min
- Numerator: $1.645 \times 1.921 + (12 - 12) = 3.160$ min
- Denominator: $\mu_2 = 12$ min
- $B_{min} = 3.160 / 12 = 0.263$

Round up: $B = 1$ pallet. With practical safety factor of 2×, **design $B = 2$ pallets**.

*(1 mark per step)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the **classification of Material Handling Systems (MHS)** used in FMS. Compare **AGVs, conveyors, and robotic transfer arms** in a decision matrix. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An FMS has an average demand rate $\lambda = 50$ parts/h. The average AGV cycle time $T_{cycle} = 180$ s. Calculate the **minimum AGV fleet size** with a safety factor $\alpha = 0.30$. If each AGV costs ₹18,00,000 and operating cost is ₹150/h, what is the **annual MHS cost** for 300 working days × 16 h/day? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of MHS (7 Marks):**

MHS in an FMS can be classified as: *(1 mark)*

1. **Conveyor Systems** — belt, roller, chain conveyors; high throughput, fixed path.
2. **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** — wire-guided, magnetic-tape, laser-guided, vision-guided; mobile, flexible routing.
3. **Robotic Transfer Arms** — articulated 6-axis or SCARA, used in cell-based MHS.
4. **Tow-line / Tractor Systems** — continuous loop, heavy payloads.
5. **Overhead Monorail / Power-and-Free** — unidirectional with gravity return.
6. **Cartesian Gantry Robots** — bridge-type, large work envelopes.

**Decision Matrix (5 marks):**

| Criterion | Weight | Conveyor | AGV | Robot Arm |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Throughput (parts/h) | 0.25 | **High (200+)** | Medium (50–80) | Medium (40–60) |
| Routing Flexibility | 0.20 | Low | **High** | Medium |
| Initial Cost (₹ Lakhs) | 0.15 | Medium (15–30) | High (15–20/AGV) | Medium (20–50) |
| Floor Space | 0.10 | High | Low | **Low** |
| Maintenance | 0.15 | Low | Medium | Medium |
| Reliability (MTBF hrs) | 0.10 | **High (1000+)** | Medium (500) | High (800) |
| Scalability | 0.05 | Low | **High** | Low |

Apply weighted scoring: Conveyor ≈ 6.5/10, AGV ≈ 7.8/10, Robot ≈ 6.0/10. **AGV wins for moderate-to-high flexibility FMS.** *(2 marks for calculation logic and 2 marks for conclusion)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: AGV Fleet Size (3 marks)**

Using the formula from Section 3.2:

$$N_{AGV} = \left\lceil \frac{\lambda \cdot T_{cycle}}{3600} \cdot (1 + \alpha) \right\rceil$$

Substitute $\lambda = 50$ parts/h, $T_{cycle} = 180$ s, $\alpha = 0.30$:

$$N_{AGV} = \left\lceil \frac{50 \times 180}{3600} \times 1.30 \right\rceil$$

Compute inside: $50 \times 180 = 9000$, then $9000 / 3600 = 2.5$.

Then: $2.5 \times 1.30 = 3.25$.

Round up: $N_{AGV} = 4$ AGVs. *(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for result)*

**Step 2: Annual MHS Cost (4 marks)**

Capital cost = 4 × ₹18,00,000 = **₹72,00,000** *(1 mark)*

Operating hours/year = 300 × 16 = 4800 h *(1 mark)*

Annual operating cost = 4 × 4800 × ₹150 = **₹28,80,000** *(1 mark)*

Assuming 5-year straight-line depreciation:
Annual depreciation = ₹72,00,000 / 5 = **₹14,40,000** *(0.5 mark)*

**Total annual MHS cost** = 14,40,000 + 28,80,000 = **₹43,20,000** *(0.5 mark for summation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in FMS Component Questions (KTU 2024 Valuation):**
>
> 1. **Conflating "FMS" with "CIM":** FMS is a *subset* of CIM, not a synonym. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for using these terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the modular pallet concept:** Any FMS answer that does not mention **standardized pallets** and **modular fixturing** loses at least **2 marks** because this is the enabler of part-mix flexibility.
> 3. **Skipping the human element:** A 14-mark answer on FMS components that omits human resources is marked down by 2–3 marks. The 5-component structure is the *first* thing examiners check.
> 4. **Mistaking MTBF for MTTR:** In availability calculation, mixing these up is a guaranteed zero on the relevant sub-part.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** "B = 1" without "pallet" or "buffer position" loses half a mark. Always annotate units.
> 6. **Not drawing the ISA-95 pyramid:** In any 7-mark sub-question on control hierarchy, the absence of a block diagram forfeits up to 2 marks. KTU examiners follow a strict "diagram-mandatory" rule.
> 7. **Confusing OMR with OEE:** OMR (Operator-Machine Ratio) is a *staffing* metric; OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) is a *performance* metric. Don't swap them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — FMS Components (Module 4):**
>
> **1. FMS Definition & Architecture**
> - FMS = Computer-controlled integrated production of moderate part variety.
> - 5 components: Workstations, MHS, Storage, Control, Human Resources.
> - 3 flexibilities: Machine, Routing, Volume.
>
> **2. Workstations**
> - CNC machine + ATC + APC + probing = complete FMS workstation.
> - Standard tool magazine size: 20–120 tools.
> - Workstation throughput time: $T_{ws} = W_q + T_s$.
> - Availability: $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
> - OMR (Operator-Machine Ratio) for FMS: 0.2–0.5.
>
> **3. Material Handling**
> - 6 major MHS types: Conveyor, AGV, Robot, Tow-line, Monorail, Gantry.
> - AGV fleet: $N = \lceil \lambda T_{cycle} (1+\alpha) / 3600 \rceil$.
> - Standard pallet sizes: 400 × 400 mm and 500 × 500 mm.
> - Modular fixturing is the *enabler* of part-mix flexibility.
>
> **4. Storage Systems**
> - Centralized AS/RS vs. Distributed Buffer.
> - AS/RS throughput: $TH = 7200 / T_{cycle}$ (dual-command per crane).
> - Buffer size (Normal distribution): $B_{min} = [1.645\sqrt{\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2} + \mu_1 - \mu_2] / \mu_2$.
> - Empirical rule: Pallet pool = 1.5 × Number of machines.
>
> **5. Computer Control**
> - ISA-95 five-level hierarchy: ERP → MES → FMS Host → DNC/CNC → Sensors.
> - FMS Host functions: routing, dispatch, tool mgmt, DNC, SFDC, maintenance.
> - Protocols: Profibus, DeviceNet, EtherCAT, OPC UA, MTConnect.
> - Little's Law: WIP = TH × Lead Time.
>
> **6. Human Resources**
> - 5:1:1 staffing rule: 5 machines : 1 operator : 1 maintenance tech.
> - 6 core roles: Manager, Production Engineer, Maintenance Tech, Quality, Tool Room, Operator.
> - Continuous training: 40–200 hrs/year depending on role.
>
> **7. Critical Formulas Quick-Fire**
> - $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$
> - $TH = N_m \times (1/t) \times A \times hours$
> - $B_{min} = [1.645 \sqrt{\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2} + \mu_1 - \mu_2] / \mu_2$
> - $N_{AGV} = \lceil \lambda T_{cycle} (1+\alpha) / 3600 \rceil$
> - $TH_{AS/RS} = 7200 / T_{cycle}$ (per crane, dual-command)
> - $OMR = N_{op} / N_{mach} \approx 0.2 \text{ to } 0.5$
> - $WIP = TH \times T_{lead}$ (Little's Law)
>
> **8. Common Exam Traps**
> - Always mention standardized pallets and modular fixtures.
> - Always draw the ISA-95 pyramid for control questions.
> - Always state units in numerical answers.
> - Distinguish OMR (staffing) from OEE (performance).
> - FMS ≠ CIM (FMS is a subset).

---
