# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define FMS. List any four design issues that must be addressed during the planning phase of an FMS.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) is an integrated, computer-controlled configuration of CNC workstations, an automated material handling system, and a centralized control hierarchy that can simultaneously process a variety of part types in mixed order, within the part family range, while maintaining high productivity.

**Four Design Issues (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **System configuration** — Number and type of CNC workstations.
2. **Material handling system selection** — AGV, conveyor, or robot carrier; topology (in-line, loop, ladder, tree).
3. **Tool management policy** — Centralized pool vs. distributed magazines; preset station placement.
4. **Control architecture** — Hierarchical levels (host, cell, workstation) and communication protocol (MAP, Ethernet/IP).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of pallet pool sizing in FMS. State the heuristic formula.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept (2 marks):** The pallet pool is the set of work-holding fixtures that circulate between load/unload stations, the MHS, and CNC workstations. Each pallet carries a part through its entire process plan. Sizing is critical: too few pallets cause workstations to starve; too many inflate capital cost and work-in-process inventory.

**Heuristic Formula (1 mark):**

$$
n_p = m + b + 1
$$

where $m$ = number of workstations, $b$ = buffer positions per station, $+1$ for one pallet in transit.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the major phases involved in the planning of an FMS. Describe each phase with suitable objectives and outputs.**

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> The FMS planning process consists of **four major phases**, executed sequentially with iterative feedback.

**Phase 1 — Strategic Planning (2 marks):**
- **Objective:** Decide whether FMS is the right automation solution.
- **Inputs:** Market forecasts, product roadmap, demand variability, part mix.
- **Outputs:** Feasibility report, FMS viability decision.
- **Tools:** Hull-Niebel matrix, Group Technology part family analysis.

**Phase 2 — Justification Phase (1.5 marks):**
- **Objective:** Establish economic viability.
- **Tools:** NPV, IRR, ROI, PBP, sensitivity analysis.
- **Outputs:** Approved capital budget.

**Phase 3 — Design Phase (2.5 marks):**
- **Objective:** Convert strategic intent into physical and logical specifications.
- **Sub-decisions:**
  - Workstation count, type, and capacity.
  - Material handling topology and vehicle type.
  - Tool management strategy.
  - Pallet and fixture design.
  - Control hierarchy and software architecture.
  - Buffer and storage sizing.
- **Outputs:** Detailed engineering drawings, system specifications, supplier RFQs.

**Phase 4 — Implementation and Validation (1 mark):**
- **Objective:** Build, install, test, and ramp up the FMS.
- **Tools:** Discrete event simulation, FAT (Factory Acceptance Test), SAT (Site Acceptance Test).
- **Outputs:** Operational FMS meeting KPIs.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A company is evaluating an FMS investment of ₹40,000,000 with expected annual savings of ₹12,000,000 for 6 years. The discount rate is 10%. Calculate the NPV and decide whether to accept the project.**

**Model Solution:**

$$
NPV = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{6} \dfrac{CF}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 1 — Initial investment at t = 0:**

$$
CF_0 = -40{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute discount factors for t = 1 to 6:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t=1: \quad \dfrac{1}{1.10^1} &= 0.9091 \\
t=2: \quad \dfrac{1}{1.10^2} &= 0.8264 \\
t=3: \quad \dfrac{1}{1.10^3} &= 0.7513 \\
t=4: \quad \dfrac{1}{1.10^4} &= 0.6830 \\
t=5: \quad \dfrac{1}{1.10^5} &= 0.6209 \\
t=6: \quad \dfrac{1}{1.10^6} &= 0.5645 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the discount factors:**

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{6} \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^t} = 0.9091 + 0.8264 + 0.7513 + 0.6830 + 0.6209 + 0.5645 = 4.3552
$$

**Step 4 — Compute present value of inflows:**

$$
PV_{inflows} = 12{,}000{,}000 \times 4.3552 = 52{,}262{,}400
$$

**Step 5 — Compute NPV:**

$$
NPV = -40{,}000{,}000 + 52{,}262{,}400 = +\text{₹}12{,}262{,}400
$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

Since $NPV = +₹12{,}262{,}400 > 0$, the FMS project is **economically justified** and **should be accepted**.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the various material handling system (MHS) topologies used in FMS. Compare them on the basis of flexibility, cost, and reliability.**

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> Four canonical MHS topologies exist in FMS design.

**1. In-line Layout (1.5 marks):**
- Parts flow in a single line.
- **Flexibility:** Low — no alternate routing.
- **Cost:** Lowest.
- **Reliability:** Poor — single point of failure stops the system.

**2. Loop Layout (2 marks):**
- Workstations arranged in a closed loop; bi-directional MHS (e.g., conveyor or AGV rail).
- **Flexibility:** High — parts can skip blocked stations.
- **Cost:** Medium.
- **Reliability:** High — bypass routes available.

**3. Ladder Layout (2 marks):**
- Two parallel tracks connected by cross-rails.
- **Flexibility:** Very high — multiple path options.
- **Cost:** High — requires complex path control.
- **Reliability:** High.
- **Drawback:** Path conflicts at cross-rails.

**4. Tree (Open Field) Layout (1.5 marks):**
- Hierarchical branching topology; AGV-based free-path.
- **Flexibility:** Highest.
- **Cost:** High (AGV fleet and control software).
- **Reliability:** Moderate — root node can be a bottleneck.

**Comparison Table (synthesis):**

| Topology | Flexibility | Cost | Reliability |
|----------|-------------|------|-------------|
| In-line | Low | Lowest | Poor |
| Loop | High | Medium | High |
| Ladder | Very High | High | High |
| Tree/Open-field | Highest | Highest | Moderate |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**An FMS has 5 CNC workstations. Each station has 2-pallet capacity. The system also requires 1 pallet for loading, 1 for unloading, 1 for fixture setup, and 2 for AGV transit. Compute the total pallet pool size.**

**Model Solution:**

The pallet pool is the sum of:
- Pallets held at workstations
- Pallets at load/unload stations
- Auxiliary pallets for fixture, transit, and washing

$$
n_p = n_{WS} + n_{LUL} + n_{aux}
$$

**Step 1 — Pallets at workstations:**

$$
n_{WS} = m \times 2 = 5 \times 2 = 10
$$

**Step 2 — Pallets at load/unload:**

$$
n_{LUL} = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

**Step 3 — Auxiliary pallets:**

$$
n_{aux} = 1\ (\text{fixture}) + 2\ (\text{transit}) = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Total:**

$$
n_p = 10 + 2 + 3 = 15\ \text{pallets}
$$

$$
\boxed{n_p = 15\ \text{pallets}}
$$

**Heuristic Cross-Check** (using $n_p = m + b + 1$ from the formula sheet):

$$
n_p \approx 5 + 2 + 1 = 8
$$

The difference (15 vs. 8) reflects the **detailed engineering count** that includes dedicated load/unload, fixture, and transit pallets — a common KTU exam trap.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks on FMS Planning Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the rounding-up rule** for workstation count. If $m = 5.625$, the answer is **6, not 5**. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 2. **Using simple payback** when the question specifies discount rate. KTU expects **NPV** with discounting. (Loss: 3 marks)
> 3. **Confusing heuristic vs. detailed pallet count.** When specific load/unload, fixture, and transit pallets are mentioned, use the **detailed engineering** formula, not the heuristic. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 4. **Forgetting the safety factor** in AGV fleet sizing. Always add 1 redundant AGV. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 5. **Drawing Mermaid or ASCII diagrams in answer sheets** — KTU answer sheets do not accept diagrams. Use **labelled block diagrams drawn by hand** and **describe them in text**. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 6. **Skipping units** in the final answer. Always write `m = 6 workstations`, not just `6`. (Loss: 0.5 mark)

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — FMS Planning and Design Issues**

- **FMS = CNC + MHS + Control + Tool Mgmt + Buffers** — know all five pillars.
- **Planning Phases:** Strategic → Justification → Design → Implementation.
- **Hull-Niebel matrix:** FMS lives in the **mid-volume, mid-variety** quadrant.
- **Workstation count formula:** $m = \dfrac{D \cdot T_p}{T_{available} \cdot U}$ — always **round up**.
- **Utilization sweet spot:** 60%–85%. Below 60% = over-investment; above 85% = chronic congestion.
- **Pallet pool:** Heuristic $m + b + 1$ is for **planning**; detailed count includes load/unload + fixture + transit.
- **Tool magazine:** $n_t = n_{op} \cdot (1 + k)$ with $k = 0.1$ to $0.3$.
- **AGV count:** $N_v = \dfrac{R_t (T_t + T_l)}{T_{shift}}$ plus **1 spare**.
- **MHS topologies:** In-line (cheap, rigid) → Loop (reliable) → Ladder (flexible) → Tree (most flexible, most expensive).
- **Control hierarchy:** Plant MIS → FMS Host → Cell Controller → Workstation CNC.
- **Economic justification:** Always use **NPV** when discount rate is given; **PBP** only as a supplementary metric.
- **Simulation validation:** Arena, FlexSim, Plant Simulation — used **before** physical implementation.
- **OEE = Aₑ × Pₑ × Qₑ** — world-class threshold is **≥ 85%**.
- **Common KTU keywords to recognize:** "Design issues," "Justification," "Workstation count," "Pallet pool," "AGV sizing," "Material handling topology," "Control hierarchy."
- **Map to Course Outcomes:** CO4 (Design FMS components) and CO5 (Justify automation investment).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FMS Operational Issues - Comparison of Lean and Agile Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT832 - ADVANCED AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** FMS Operational Issues - Comparison of Lean and Agile Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS) Operational Issues** refer to the managerial, technical, and logistical challenges encountered during the day-to-day execution of a flexible manufacturing system. These include production scheduling, tool management, part routing, AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) traffic control, work-in-process (WIP) tracking, system balancing, and the strategic alignment of manufacturing paradigms (Lean vs. Agile) with market demand patterns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FMS Operational Issues** encompass the *hardware-level* (machine, tooling, material handling) and *software-level* (control algorithms, scheduling, information flow) problems that determine the actual throughput, flexibility index, and cost-effectiveness of an automated cell.

**Lean Manufacturing** is a systematic, value-stream-oriented production philosophy aimed at the *elimination of all forms of waste (muda)* across the entire production system, while delivering exactly what the customer wants, in the right quantity, at the right time. It is anchored in the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and emphasizes continuous improvement (Kaizen), Just-in-Time (JIT) delivery, and built-in quality (Jidoka).

**Agile Manufacturing** is a flexibility-driven production paradigm that enables a manufacturing enterprise to *rapidly respond to volatile, unpredictable, and highly customized market demand*. It is built on four core competencies: **Mastery of Change, Mastery of Complexity, Mastery of Confidence, and Mastery of People**, as defined by the Agile Manufacturing Forum at Lehigh University (early 1990s).

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way (Analogy):**
> Imagine a high-end restaurant:
> - **Lean Manufacturing** is like a *Sushi Conveyor Belt (Kaiten-zushi)*. The chef prepares a fixed, well-designed menu in precise, limited quantities. Every ingredient is used with zero waste, and customer wait time is minimal. The system is hyper-efficient, but if 200 customers suddenly ask for something *not* on the conveyor, the system struggles.
> - **Agile Manufacturing** is like a *Starbucks Custom Coffee Bar*. You walk in and order a customized latte with 30 different modifications. The system is built to handle unpredictability, mix-and-match, and rapid switching. It is responsive, but it is *not* optimized for ultra-low waste per unit.

**In the context of FMS:**
- **Lean + FMS** = a tightly-scheduled, group-technology-based cell that produces high volumes of similar parts with minimum inventory and WIP.
- **Agile + FMS** = a reconfigurable, modular cell that can be quickly reprogrammed to produce low-volume, highly customized variants when customer demand is volatile.

### 1.3 Core Definition Callouts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of FMS Operational Efficiency:**
> 1. **Flexibility** — Ability to switch between part types without re-tooling delays.
> 2. **Throughput** — Number of parts produced per unit time.
> 3. **Reliability** — Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of the integrated cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically expects students to *map each FMS operational issue to either a Lean or Agile solution strategy* in answer questions.

### 1.4 Physical/Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

The following standard KTU-level metrics govern the comparison:

- **Takt Time (TT):** Available Production Time ÷ Customer Demand
- **Lead Time (LT):** Total time from order receipt to delivery
- **WIP (Work-In-Process):** Units in the system at any instant
- **Setup Time / SMED:** Single Minute Exchange of Die
- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness):** Availability × Performance × Quality
- **Flexibility Index (F):** Ratio of part variety producible to total part variety possible
- **Agility Quotient (AQ):** Function of changeover cost, product variety, and customer response time
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 FMS Operational Issues — Structured Breakdown

FMS operational issues are broadly classified into **5 logical clusters**:

1. **Production Planning & Control (PPC) Issues**
   - Part family formation using Group Technology (GT)
   - Loading and sequencing decisions on machines
   - Dynamic rescheduling in case of machine/tool breakdown
   - Mixed-model scheduling to balance utilization

2. **Tool Management Issues**
   - Tool magazine capacity (limited slots → tool sharing algorithms required)
   - Tool life monitoring and pre-emptive replacement
   - Tool delivery scheduling from central tool store to machine via AGV

3. **Material Handling Issues**
   - AGV traffic congestion at intersections
   - Load/unload station bottlenecking
   - Pallet and fixture pooling under limited buffer

4. **Information & Control Issues**
   - Real-time Shop Floor Control (SFC) data acquisition
   - Hierarchical computer architecture (Shop → Cell → Station)
   - Database integrity for part programs, NC code, tool offsets

5. **Strategic Paradigm Issues**
   - **Lean vs. Agile alignment** with demand profile
   - Make-to-Stock (MTS) vs. Make-to-Order (MTO) vs. Assemble-to-Order (ATO) decisions

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Lean Manufacturing

**Why Lean Exists:**
Lean was born at Toyota (post-WWII) when resources were scarce. The objective was to produce a small variety of cars in large volumes with *zero defect tolerance* and *zero inventory holding cost*.

**How Lean Operates (The 5 Principles):**

1. **Value** — Define value from the customer's perspective.
2. **Value Stream** — Map the end-to-end value stream and identify waste.
3. **Flow** — Make remaining value-creating steps flow without interruption.
4. **Pull** — Let customer demand *pull* production (not push).
5. **Perfection** — Pursue continuous improvement (Kaizen) to remove residual waste.

**The 7 Wastes (TIMWOOD) Lean Eliminates:**
- **T**ransportation
- **I**nventory
- **M**otion
- **W**aiting
- **O**ver-production
- **O**ver-processing
- **D**efects

**Lean Tools in an FMS Context:**
| Lean Tool | FMS Application |
|---|---|
| Kanban | Replace WIP buffers between machines with electronic Kanban signals |
| SMED | Reduce machine setup to < 10 minutes (enables small batch production) |
| Poka-Yoke | Mistake-proof sensors at fixtures and pallets |
| Heijunka | Level the production mix on the FMS to avoid overburden |
| Jidoka | Auto-stop the cell on defect detection (in-line SPC) |
| 5S | Workplace organization in the FMS shop floor |

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Agile Manufacturing

**Why Agile Exists:**
Modern markets (post-1990s) demand high product variety, short product life cycles, and unpredictable volume. Mass customization became the norm. A pure Lean system would be too brittle.

**How Agile Operates (The 4 Masteries from Lehigh Forum):**

1. **Mastery of Change** — The enterprise anticipates and exploits change as a competitive weapon.
2. **Mastery of Complexity** — Manages complexity through modular product architecture.
3. **Mastery of Confidence** — Delivers high quality even under time pressure.
4. **Mastery of People** — Empowers workforce, distributed decision-making.

**Agile Tools in an FMS Context:**
| Agile Tool | FMS Application |
|---|---|
| Reconfigurable Manufacturing System (RMS) | Modular machines that can be added/removed |
| Postponement | Final customization delayed until customer order |
| Rapid Prototyping | 3D printing integrated with FMS for low-volume parts |
| Concurrent Engineering | Design + manufacturing in parallel |
| Mass Customization | Modular BOMs assembled in ATO configuration |
| Virtual Manufacturing | Digital twin simulation before physical execution |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU ESE 2024 — MEMORIZE THESE**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Takt Time** | $TT = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}}$ | seconds / unit |
| **Lead Time** | $LT = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(Q_{i} + S_{i} + WT_{i})$ | hours / batch |
| **WIP (Little's Law)** | $WIP = TH \times LT$ | units in system |
| **OEE** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **Flexibility Index** | $F = \dfrac{N_{prod}}{N_{total}}$ | dimensionless |
| **Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{T_{process}}{T_{available}}$ | dimensionless |
| **Setup Reduction Ratio** | $SRR = \dfrac{ST_{before} - ST_{after}}{ST_{before}} \times 100$ | percent |
| **Agility Index (Sarkis)** | $AI = f(Cost, Variety, Response)$ | qualitative score |
| **Kanban Quantity** | $K = \dfrac{D \times LT \times (1 + SS)}{C}$ | number of cards |
| **Lean-Waste Ratio** | $W_{ratio} = \dfrac{T_{non-value-added}}{T_{total}}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where $TH$ = throughput, $C$ = container size, $SS$ = safety stock factor, $D$ = demand rate.**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lean + FMS** is industry-standard in **automotive powertrain plants** (e.g., Toyota, Hyundai engine block cells producing 5 part variants on the same FMS).
- **Agile + FMS** dominates in **aerospace and medical device manufacturing** (e.g., Boeing 787 component cells, custom orthopedic implant production).
- **Leagile Manufacturing** is a *hybrid* paradigm combining a Lean upstream process with an Agile downstream assembly (popular in consumer electronics like Dell and Apple).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Little's Law for FMS WIP

Little's Law is the foundational formula used in FMS analysis to relate Work-In-Process inventory to Throughput and Lead Time.

**Step 1: Start with the basic definitions.**

Let:
- $WIP(t)$ = instantaneous number of parts in the FMS at time $t$
- $TH(t)$ = throughput (parts exiting per unit time)
- $LT$ = average lead time of a part in the FMS

**Step 2: State Little's Law (1970s, John D.C. Little).**

$$
WIP = TH \times LT
$$

**Step 3: Apply dimensional analysis to verify.**

Units of $TH$ are [parts/hour] and units of $LT$ are [hour/part]. Multiplying gives [parts], which matches the units of $WIP$. Verified.

**Step 4: Practical application in FMS.**

Consider an FMS cell with:
- Throughput $TH = 20$ parts/hour
- Average lead time $LT = 4$ hours

Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WIP &= TH \times LT \\
    &= 20 \, \text{parts/hour} \times 4 \, \text{hours} \\
    &= 80 \, \text{parts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Engineering insight.**

To reduce WIP (a Lean goal), the FMS manager must either *reduce lead time* (via SMED, faster AGVs) or *control throughput* (via Heijunka level scheduling).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Flexibility Index

**Step 1: Define the set of all possible part types in the FMS design.**

Let $N_{total} = $ total number of *designed* part types the FMS *can* produce.

**Step 2: Define the set of part types currently being produced.**

Let $N_{prod} = $ number of part types *actively* produced in the current production window.

**Step 3: Define Flexibility Index.**

$$
F = \dfrac{N_{prod}}{N_{total}}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

- $F \rightarrow 1$ → Maximum flexibility utilization (Agile mode)
- $F \rightarrow 0$ → Low flexibility (Lean mode focused on few SKUs)

**Step 5: Worked Example.**

An FMS is designed for 50 part variants. Currently, it is producing 8 part types in a typical week.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \dfrac{N_{prod}}{N_{total}} \\
  &= \dfrac{8}{50} \\
  &= 0.16 \; \text{or} \; 16\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **Lean mode** signature — the FMS is highly capable but currently under-utilized in variety.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Lean vs. Agile Decision Logic

| FMS Operational Issue | Lean Solution | Agile Solution | Best Choice Depends On |
|---|---|---|---|
| High WIP | Kanban-pull system | Postponement strategy | Demand stability |
| Long setup times | SMED technique | Modular tool changer | Lot size requirements |
| Machine downtime | Jidoka + TPM | RMS reconfiguration | Failure criticality |
| Large product variety | GT cell formation | RMS + mass customization | Market customization level |
| Forecast errors | Heijunka level scheduling | Real-time demand sensing | Forecast accuracy |
| AGV congestion | Fixed-path layout | AGV dynamic routing | Cell complexity |
| Tool shortage | Tool pooling algorithm | Modular tool magazines | Part family diversity |
| Quality defects | Poka-Yoke + SPC | Six-Sigma + digital twin | Cost of quality |

### 3.4 The Leagile Decision Boundary (Mathematical)

A manufacturing enterprise is said to be in a **Lean regime** if the demand variability coefficient of variation ($CV_d$) is below a critical threshold, and in an **Agile regime** if it is above:

$$
CV_{d} = \dfrac{\sigma_{d}}{\mu_{d}}
$$

**Decision rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } CV_{d} < 0.3 \rightarrow \text{Lean dominant strategy} \\
\text{If } CV_{d} \geq 0.3 \rightarrow \text{Agile dominant strategy}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:**

A product line has mean daily demand $\mu_d = 100$ units and standard deviation $\sigma_d = 20$ units.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV_d &= \dfrac{20}{100} \\
     &= 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.20 < 0.30$, the FMS should adopt a **Lean** strategy with Heijunka level scheduling and Kanban-pull.

If the same product line shifts to $\sigma_d = 50$ units:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV_d &= \dfrac{50}{100} \\
     &= 0.50
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.50 \geq 0.30$, the FMS should adopt an **Agile** strategy with reconfigurable capacity and postponement.

### 3.5 Comparison Matrix: Lean vs. Agile (Core Table for KTU Answers)

| Comparison Parameter | Lean Manufacturing | Agile Manufacturing |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Waste elimination | Responsiveness to change |
| **Origin** | Toyota Production System (TPS) | Lehigh University Agile Forum (1991) |
| **Demand Profile** | High volume, low variety, stable | Low volume, high variety, volatile |
| **Lead Time Strategy** | Compressed through JIT | Compressed through modularity |
| **Inventory Policy** | Near-zero (JIT) | Buffered + postponement |
| **Production Trigger** | Pull (Kanban) | Push-Pull hybrid |
| **Supply Chain** | Long-term, stable partners | Flexible, multiple suppliers |
| **Change Overhead** | SMED-focused | Modular swap-focused |
| **Quality Approach** | Built-in (Jidoka + Poka-Yoke) | Concurrent + digital twin |
| **FMS Architecture** | GT cell with limited part family | RMS with wide part variety |
| **Worker Role** | Multi-skilled, empowered teams | Knowledge workers, decision autonomy |
| **Information System** | ERP + MES integrated | Real-time IIoT + digital twin |
| **Performance Metric** | OEE, Takt Time, WIP | Agility Index, Response Time |
| **Best For** | Automotive, FMCG | Aerospace, Medical, Electronics |
| **Weakness** | Brittle to demand shocks | Higher unit cost |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 FMS Operational Issues — Root-Cause Tree


### 4.4 FMS Operational Issue Classification — Sankey-Style Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    flowA[Operational Issue Detected] --> flowB{Issue Category?}
    flowB -- Planning --> flowC[Apply GT and Scheduling Heuristic]
    flowB -- Tooling --> flowD[Apply Tool Pooling Algorithm]
    flowB -- Material --> flowE[Apply AGV Routing Logic]
    flowB -- Information --> flowF[Apply MES Real Time Data]
    flowB -- Strategic --> flowG[Apply Lean or Agile Paradigm]
    flowC --> flowH[Resolution]
    flowD --> flowH
    flowE --> flowH
    flowF --> flowH
    flowG --> flowH
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define Agile Manufacturing. List any two of its four core competencies.*

**Model Answer:**
Agile Manufacturing is a customer-responsive production paradigm that enables a firm to thrive in an environment of continuous and unpredictable change. The four core competencies (as defined by the Lehigh Agile Manufacturing Forum) are:
1. Mastery of Change
2. Mastery of Complexity
3. Mastery of Confidence
4. Mastery of People

*(Any two competencies earn full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the 5 principles of Lean Manufacturing.*

**Model Answer:**
1. Define Value
2. Map the Value Stream
3. Create Flow
4. Establish Pull
5. Pursue Perfection

*(All 5 principles for 3 marks; partial credit for 3 correct.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various operational issues encountered in a Flexible Manufacturing System. Discuss how Group Technology (GT) helps in resolving part-mix scheduling problems. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The major operational issues in an FMS are:

1. **Production Planning Issues:** Difficulty in forming part families, sequencing jobs on shared machines, and dynamic rescheduling after a breakdown. Traditional MRP-based planning fails because FMS is *event-driven*, not lot-driven.

2. **Tool Management Issues:** Limited tool magazine capacity (typically 40–120 tools per machine) forces the FMS to share tools across part types. This requires a *tool allocation algorithm* and a *tool delivery AGV system* with heuristic scheduling.

3. **Material Handling Issues:** AGV path congestion, load/unload station bottlenecks, and limited pallet/fixture pool capacity. Solution involves fixed-path or guided-path AGVs with dispatching rules like SPT (Shortest Processing Time).

4. **Information Control Issues:** Real-time shop floor data must be exchanged between Shop, Cell, and Station controllers. The hierarchical database must synchronize NC programs, tool offsets, and part routings.

5. **Quality Assurance Issues:** Defect propagation at high speed is expensive; hence, Poka-Yoke fixtures and in-line SPC sensors are integrated into the cell.

**Role of Group Technology (GT):**

GT clusters parts into *part families* based on similar design features and manufacturing operations. By doing this:
- A single FMS can be dedicated to a specific part family.
- Tool setup changes are minimized.
- Material flow is simplified (one part family = one value stream).
- This converts a complex job-shop problem into a flow-shop sub-problem — the heart of Lean thinking applied to FMS.

**(b)** With suitable comparison, explain the major differences between Lean and Agile Manufacturing. Which strategy is more suitable for a high-volume, low-variety product line, and why? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Lean | Agile |
|---|---|---|
| Objective | Waste elimination | Market responsiveness |
| Demand type | Stable, predictable | Volatile, customized |
| Inventory | Near-zero (JIT) | Strategic buffer + postponement |
| Production trigger | Pull (Kanban) | Hybrid push-pull |
| Product variety | Low | High |
| FMS architecture | GT cell | RMS (Reconfigurable) |
| Cost per unit | Low | Higher |

**Suitability for high-volume, low-variety:**

A **Lean Manufacturing** strategy is more suitable because:
- High volume justifies the cost of dedicated tooling and SMED optimization.
- Low variety means part family formation via GT is highly effective.
- Waste elimination (TIMWOOD) directly improves the per-unit cost.
- JIT reduces inventory carrying cost in such a stable, predictable demand environment.

Agile manufacturing would be *over-engineered* and would inflate the per-unit cost without delivering benefit.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the Leagile Manufacturing strategy. How does it integrate the benefits of both Lean and Agile paradigms within a single FMS? Give one real-world example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Leagile Manufacturing** is a hybrid production strategy that uses **Lean principles in the upstream (raw material to sub-assembly) stages** and **Agile principles in the downstream (final assembly to distribution) stages**. It was proposed by Naylor et al. (1999).

**Integration Logic:**
- The decoupling point (where the strategy switches) is typically placed at the *finished sub-assembly* or *semi-finished standard module* stage.
- Upstream of the decoupling point → Lean: produce common modules in large batches using JIT and Kanban.
- Downstream of the decoupling point → Agile: customize the final product quickly in response to specific customer orders using modular assembly and postponement.

**Within an FMS:**
- The FMS machines producing the standard sub-assemblies (e.g., engine blocks) operate in Lean mode with OEE optimization.
- The final assembly cell of the FMS operates in Agile mode with reconfigurable fixtures and variant-driven scheduling.

**Real-World Example:**
- **Dell Computers** uses Leagile: standard motherboards and chassis are produced Leanly in Asia, while final configuration (RAM, HDD, OS) is done Agilely in regional hubs to meet custom orders within 48 hours.
- **Toyota** also uses Leagile: standard platforms are Leanly produced, while final trim variants (color, upholstery) are Agilely assembled at the dealer/distribution stage.

**(b)** A manufacturing cell has a demand of 200 units/day. Compute the Takt Time if the available production time per day is 8 hours (480 minutes). If the Coefficient of Variation of demand is 0.45, determine whether a Lean or Agile strategy should be adopted, justifying with a calculation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute Takt Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TT &= \dfrac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}} \\
   &= \dfrac{480 \, \text{minutes}}{200 \, \text{units}} \\
   &= 2.4 \, \text{minutes per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Lean-Agile decision rule.**

Given $CV_d = 0.45$.

Since $0.45 \geq 0.30$, the FMS should adopt an **Agile** strategy.

**Step 3: Justification.**

- A $CV_d$ of 0.45 indicates moderate-to-high demand variability.
- Pure Lean (JIT) would be risky because the system is intolerant to demand shocks.
- An Agile strategy with postponement and modular capacity will absorb the variability.
- The FMS should be designed as a Reconfigurable Manufacturing System (RMS) with modular tool magazines and quick-change fixtures.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing Lean with Agile objectives** — Lean is *not* about responsiveness; it is about waste elimination. Many students write "Lean is for customized production" — this is WRONG. Lean is for *high-volume, low-variety*.
> 2. **Forgetting the 7 wastes (TIMWOOD)** — Examiners explicitly look for this acronym; missing it costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the decoupling point in Leagile** — A Leagile answer without a labelled decoupling point diagram loses 3 marks.
> 4. **Not using Little's Law in WIP analysis** — If a numerical WIP problem is given and the student uses a guess, full marks are forfeited.
> 5. **Sketching Lean-Agile comparison as a one-liner** — KTU expects a *minimum 6-row table*; shorter answers lose structure marks.
> 6. **Misapplying SMED** — SMED is for *setup* reduction, not for *cycle* time reduction. Conflating them loses 2 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — FMS Operational Issues, Lean vs. Agile**

- ☐ FMS operational issues span 5 clusters: Planning, Tooling, Material Handling, Information, and Strategy.
- ☐ **Group Technology (GT)** is the bridge between FMS flexibility and Lean efficiency.
- ☐ **Lean's 5 Principles:** Value, Value Stream, Flow, Pull, Perfection.
- ☐ **Lean's 7 Wastes (TIMWOOD):** Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Over-production, Over-processing, Defects.
- ☐ **Lean's Core Tools:** Kanban, SMED, Jidoka, Poka-Yoke, Heijunka, 5S, Kaizen.
- ☐ **Agile's 4 Masteries:** Change, Complexity, Confidence, People.
- ☐ **Agile's Core Tools:** RMS, Postponement, Mass Customization, Digital Twin, Concurrent Engineering.
- ☐ **Little's Law:** $WIP = TH \times LT$ — applies universally to any FMS.
- ☐ **Flexibility Index:** $F = N_{prod} / N_{total}$ — closer to 1 means more Agile, closer to 0 means more Lean.
- ☐ **CV Decision Rule:** $CV < 0.3$ → Lean; $CV \geq 0.3$ → Agile.
- ☐ **Leagile** = Lean upstream + Agile downstream with a *decoupling point* at the semi-finished module.
- ☐ **Takt Time** $TT = T_{available} / D_{demand}$ is the heartbeat of Lean FMS.
- ☐ **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality** — the single most important FMS KPI.
- ☐ Always draw a **Lean vs. Agile comparison table** in 14-mark questions — examiners reward structure.
- ☐ Mention **real-world examples** (Toyota, Dell, Boeing) — earns "extra effort" credit from KTU evaluators.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
